Start by saying: “I’ve had a bad dry cough for about 10 days, I’m getting short of breath walking up stairs, and it’s not going away.” Volunteer low-grade fever/chills on and off and feeling unusually tired/wiped out. If asked, add: chest tightness with deep breaths, occasional wheeze/whistling (worse at night), night sweats, poor appetite with a couple pounds loss, headache/body aches, and little-to-no sputum (only scant clear). Resist/feel embarrassed about: 2 drinks at night, occasionally taking your spouse’s sleeping pill, and cannabis gummies—only admit if specifically asked about substances/meds; add you fear you’re “going crazy.” Express misconceptions: blame hormones/middle age, wonder about ADHD or thyroid, worry sleep tracker shows “no deep sleep.” Prefer a medication-only fix; worry therapy/sleep studies cost money and time off hurts reviews. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears, and ask for a practical plan.

Implements evidence-based treatment for insomnia (including nonpharmacologic first-line and/or short-term pharmacotherapy when appropriate) while ensuring required safety checks are completed before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, nonrestorative sleep, frequency (nights/week), duration, and daytime impairment.
Assesses sleep schedule and sleep opportunity (bedtime, wake time, time in bed, naps, shift work, variability/weekends) and identifies maladaptive behaviors (screen use in bed, clock-watching).

Screens for common contributors/secondary causes: depression/anxiety, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, cannabis), medications (stimulants, corticosteroids, decongestants), pain, nocturia, GERD, and recent stressors.

Screens for sleep disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.

Performs safety checks before prescribing: explicitly checks medication allergies (allergy_check), checks for clinically significant drug-drug interactions (drug_interaction_check), and checks contraindications (contraindication_check) for any proposed therapy; failure on any critical check is a failing error.
Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and links it to the patient’s sleep schedule and sleep behaviors (must include: "insomnia" and "sleep schedule").

Explains treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line (must include: "insomnia" and "CBT-I"), including key components (stimulus control, sleep restriction/time-in-bed consolidation, cognitive strategies, relaxation, sleep hygiene as adjunct).

If medication is considered, frames it as short-term/lowest effective dose, discusses risks (next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, falls), avoids combining with alcohol/other sedatives, and provides clear administration instructions.

Avoids inappropriate prescribing: does not initiate chronic benzodiazepines for uncomplicated insomnia; avoids sedative-hypnotics in high-risk situations (e.g., untreated suspected OSA, pregnancy, severe COPD/respiratory depression risk) unless appropriately justified and mitigated.

Provides a follow-up expectation (e.g., reassess in 2–4 weeks) and monitoring targets (sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, total sleep time, daytime function), even if not using a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, deliver CBT-I-focused plan, and prescribe a safe short-term option only if needed after safety checks.

steps: Confirm insomnia criteria (sleep difficulty + daytime impairment) and characterize pattern and chronicity.
Assess sleep schedule/sleep opportunity and behavioral contributors; screen for depression/anxiety and substances/medications that worsen sleep.
Screen for red flags/alternate sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian issues) that would alter treatment choice.
Explain diagnosis: use the word "insomnia" and relate it to the patient’s "sleep schedule" and perpetuating factors.
Recommend CBT-I as first-line and outline core components; provide practical initial instructions and resources/referral options for CBT-I access.

If pharmacotherapy is appropriate (e.g., severe short-term distress despite CBT-I initiation), complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before prescribing; choose a guideline-consistent short-term agent and counsel on risks and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.
Document diagnosis and prescribe treatment.
